# frozen_string_literal: true

module Langchain
  class Assistant
    module Messages
      class OllamaMessage < Base
        # OpenAI uses the following roles:
        ROLES = [
          "system",
          "assistant",
          "user",
          "tool"
        ].freeze

        TOOL_ROLE = "tool"

        # Initialize a new OpenAI message
        #
        # @param role [String] The role of the message
        # @param content [String] The content of the message
        # @param image_url [String] The URL of the image to include in the message
        # @param tool_calls [Array<Hash>] The tool calls made in the message
        # @param tool_call_id [String] The ID of the tool call
        def initialize(role:, content: nil, image_url: nil, tool_calls: [], tool_call_id: nil)
          raise ArgumentError, "Role must be one of #{ROLES.join(", ")}" unless ROLES.include?(role)
          raise ArgumentError, "Tool calls must be an array of hashes" unless tool_calls.is_a?(Array) && tool_calls.all? { |tool_call| tool_call.is_a?(Hash) }

          # Ensure compatibility with `URI::RFC2396_PARSER` (Ruby 3.4+) and `URI::DEFAULT_PARSER` (<= 3.3).
          uri_parser = defined?(URI::RFC2396_PARSER) ? URI::RFC2396_PARSER : URI::DEFAULT_PARSER
          raise ArgumentError, "image_url must be a valid url" if image_url && !uri_parser.make_regexp.match?(image_url)

          @role = role
          # Some Tools return content as a JSON hence `.to_s`
          @content = content.to_s
          @image_url = image_url
          @tool_calls = tool_calls
          @tool_call_id = tool_call_id
        end

        # Convert the message to an OpenAI API-compatible hash
        #
        # @return [Hash] The message as an OpenAI API-compatible hash
        def to_hash
          {}.tap do |h|
            h[:role] = role
            h[:content] = content if content # Content is nil for tool calls
            h[:images] = [image.base64] if image
            h[:tool_calls] = tool_calls if tool_calls.any?
            h[:tool_call_id] = tool_call_id if tool_call_id
          end
        end

        # Check if the message came from an LLM
        #
        # @return [Boolean] true/false whether this message was produced by an LLM
        def llm?
          assistant?
        end

        # Check if the message came from an LLM
        #
        # @return [Boolean] true/false whether this message was produced by an LLM
        def assistant?
          role == "assistant"
        end

        # Check if the message are system instructions
        #
        # @return [Boolean] true/false whether this message are system instructions
        def system?
          role == "system"
        end

        # Check if the message is a tool call
        #
        # @return [Boolean] true/false whether this message is a tool call
        def tool?
          role == "tool"
        end
      end
    end
  end
end

# frozen_string_literal: true

module Langchain
  class Assistant
    module Messages
      class MistralAIMessage < Base
        # MistralAI uses the following roles:
        ROLES = [
          "system",
          "assistant",
          "user",
          "tool"
        ].freeze

        TOOL_ROLE = "tool"

        # Initialize a new MistralAI message
        #
        # @param role [String] The role of the message
        # @param content [String] The content of the message
        # @param image_url [String] The URL of the image
        # @param tool_calls [Array<Hash>] The tool calls made in the message
        # @param tool_call_id [String] The ID of the tool call
        def initialize(role:, content: nil, image_url: nil, tool_calls: [], tool_call_id: nil) # TODO: Implement image_file: reference (https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference/messages/object#messages/object-content)
          raise ArgumentError, "Role must be one of #{ROLES.join(", ")}" unless ROLES.include?(role)
          raise ArgumentError, "Tool calls must be an array of hashes" unless tool_calls.is_a?(Array) && tool_calls.all? { |tool_call| tool_call.is_a?(Hash) }

          @role = role
          # Some Tools return content as a JSON hence `.to_s`
          @content = content.to_s
          # Make sure you're using the Pixtral model if you want to send image_url
          @image_url = image_url
          @tool_calls = tool_calls
          @tool_call_id = tool_call_id
        end

        # Check if the message came from an LLM
        #
        # @return [Boolean] true/false whether this message was produced by an LLM
        def llm?
          assistant?
        end

        # Convert the message to an MistralAI API-compatible hash
        #
        # @return [Hash] The message as an MistralAI API-compatible hash
        def to_hash
          if assistant?
            assistant_hash
          elsif system?
            system_hash
          elsif tool?
            tool_hash
          elsif user?
            user_hash
          end
        end

        # Check if the message came from an LLM
        #
        # @return [Boolean] true/false whether this message was produced by an LLM
        def assistant?
          role == "assistant"
        end

        # Check if the message are system instructions
        #
        # @return [Boolean] true/false whether this message are system instructions
        def system?
          role == "system"
        end

        # Check if the message is a tool call
        #
        # @return [Boolean] true/false whether this message is a tool call
        def tool?
          role == "tool"
        end

        # Convert the message to an MistralAI API-compatible hash
        # @return [Hash] The message as an MistralAI API-compatible hash, with the role as "assistant"
        def assistant_hash
          {
            role: "assistant",
            content: content,
            tool_calls: tool_calls,
            prefix: false
          }
        end

        # Convert the message to an MistralAI API-compatible hash
        # @return [Hash] The message as an MistralAI API-compatible hash, with the role as "system"
        def system_hash
          {
            role: "system",
            content: build_content_array
          }
        end

        # Convert the message to an MistralAI API-compatible hash
        # @return [Hash] The message as an MistralAI API-compatible hash, with the role as "tool"
        def tool_hash
          {
            role: "tool",
            content: content,
            tool_call_id: tool_call_id
          }
        end

        # Convert the message to an MistralAI API-compatible hash
        # @return [Hash] The message as an MistralAI API-compatible hash, with the role as "user"
        def user_hash
          {
            role: "user",
            content: build_content_array
          }
        end

        # Builds the content value for the message hash
        # @return [Array<Hash>] An array of content hashes, with keys :type and :text or :image_url.
        def build_content_array
          content_details = []

          if content && !content.empty?
            content_details << {
              type: "text",
              text: content
            }
          end

          if image_url
            content_details << {
              type: "image_url",
              image_url: image_url
            }
          end

          content_details
        end
      end
    end
  end
end

# frozen_string_literal: true

module Langchain::LLM::Response
  class AnthropicResponse < BaseResponse
    def model
      raw_response[:model]
    end

    def completion
      completions.first
    end

    def chat_completion
      chat_completion = chat_completions&.find { |h| h[:type].to_s == "text" }
      chat_completion && chat_completion[:text]
    end

    def tool_calls
      tool_call = chat_completions&.find { |h| h[:type].to_s == "tool_use" }
      tool_call ? [tool_call.to_h] : []
    end

    def chat_completions
      raw_response[:content]
    end

    def completions
      [raw_response[:completion]]
    end

    def stop_reason
      raw_response[:stop_reason]
    end

    def stop_sequence
      raw_response[:stop_sequence]
    end

    def log_id
      raw_response[:id]
    end

    def prompt_tokens
      raw_response[:usage][:input_tokens].to_i
    end

    def completion_tokens
      raw_response[:usage][:output_tokens].to_i
    end

    def total_tokens
      prompt_tokens + completion_tokens
    end

    def role
      raw_response[:role].to_s
    end
  end
end

I now have a complete, verified picture across all layers. Here are the findings, step by step.

## Step 1 — Orientation

**Project:** `langchainrb` — a Ruby gem for building LLM-backed apps. It's mounted as a Rails engine using Zeitwerk autoloading: `lib/langchain/engine.rb:5` (`config.autoload_paths << root.join("lib")`), and the entrypoint `lib/langchain.rb:1-3` only requires `version` + `engine`. **Consequence: there is no central require/registry file** — classes load by name→path convention, so the only explicit wiring point for a provider is the adapter selector (Step 6).

**Map of the relevant layers:**
- Raw LLM clients — `lib/langchain/llm/*.rb` (base `llm/base.rb`)
- Response wrappers — `lib/langchain/llm/response/*.rb` (base `response/base_response.rb`)
- The assistant — `lib/langchain/assistant.rb`
- Provider integration (adapters) — `lib/langchain/assistant/llm/adapter.rb` (selector) + `adapters/*.rb` (base `adapters/base.rb`)
- Per-provider message forms — `lib/langchain/assistant/messages/*.rb` (base `messages/base.rb`)
- Tool-schema wire formatting — `lib/langchain/tool_definition.rb` (`FunctionSchemas`, line 79)
- Specs mirror the tree under `spec/lib/langchain/...`

## Step 2 — Trace: assistant → provider tool-calling turn

Order of execution for one turn:
1. `Assistant#run` `assistant.rb:135` → loops `handle_state` `:143`
2. `handle_state` `:249` → `process_latest_message` `:261` → `handle_user_or_tool_message` `:302`
3. `:303` calls `chat_with_llm` `:338`
4. `chat_with_llm:341` → `@llm_adapter.build_chat_params(...)` (provider-specific request formatting), then `:348` `@llm.chat(**params, &@block)`
5. **Adapter selection happened earlier**: `initialize:57` → `LLM::Adapter.build(llm)` `adapter.rb:8`, which dispatches by `llm.is_a?(...)` at `adapter.rb:9-23`
6. The client `chat` runs and wraps the raw response, e.g. `openai.rb:122` → returns `OpenAIResponse.new(response)` `openai.rb:148`
7. Back at `handle_user_or_tool_message:305`, results are read via `response.role`, `response.chat_completion`, `response.tool_calls`; tokens via `:306`
8. `set_state_for:311` — `response.tool_calls.any?` → `:in_progress`; loop re-enters and `execute_tools:327` → `run_tools:354` → `run_tool:365`
9. `run_tool:366` calls `@llm_adapter.extract_tool_call_args(tool_call:)` — **reads the provider's tool-call shape back**; the tool output is appended with role `@llm_adapter.tool_role` (`:379`/`submit_tool_output:178/:180`)

## Step 3 — Adapter family and its contract

**Shared base** `lib/langchain/assistant/llm/adapters/base.rb` declares 5 abstract methods (`raise NotImplementedError`):
- `build_chat_params` `:16` — format the request
- `extract_tool_call_args` `:30` — read tool calls back
- `build_message` `:42` — build the per-provider message
- `support_system_message?` `:49` — quirk flag
- `tool_role` `:56` — role used to return tool output

**Plus 3 methods every concrete adapter must define even though the base omits them** (they're called from `assistant.rb`, so a miss is a silent break):
- `allowed_tool_choices` — used `assistant.rb:229`
- `available_tool_names(tools)` — used `assistant.rb:410`
- `build_tools(tools)` + a private `build_tool_choice`/`build_tool_config` — used inside `build_chat_params`

**Concrete adapters and how each differs:**
- **OpenAI** `adapters/openai.rb` — OpenAI wire format (`to_openai_format` `:68`); `tool_choice` → `{"type"=>"function",...}` `:91`; allowed `["auto","none"]`; passes `parallel_tool_calls` (nil-safe `:29`); system supported.
- **Anthropic** `adapters/anthropic.rb` — `to_anthropic_format` `:58`; instructions via `params[:system]` `:28`; `tool_choice` object carries `disable_parallel_tool_use` `:85`; allowed `["auto","any"]`; `extract` reads symbol keys `:id/:name/:input` `:48-53`.
- **AwsBedrockAnthropic** `adapters/aws_bedrock_anthropic.rb` — subclass of Anthropic; **only** overrides private `build_tool_choice` `:13` (no parallel support).
- **GoogleGemini** `adapters/google_gemini.rb` — handles Gemini *and* VertexAI; warns parallel/image unsupported `:23,:43`; `tool_choice` → `function_calling_config` `:88`; `extract` reads `"functionCall"` `:52`.
- **MistralAI** `adapters/mistralai.rb` — OpenAI-format tools; warns parallel unsupported `:23`.
- **Ollama** `adapters/ollama.rb` — OpenAI-format tools; warns parallel *and* tool_choice unsupported `:23-24`; no `build_tool_choice`.

## Step 4 — Per-provider message & response families

**Message base** `messages/base.rb`: `attr_reader` role/content/image_url/tool_calls/tool_call_id; abstract `llm?` `:23`, `tool?` `:30`, `system?` `:37`; provides `user?` `:16`, `standard_role` `:44`, `image` `:54`. Each subclass also owns `ROLES`, `TOOL_ROLE`, `initialize` (role validation), and `to_hash`.

| Provider | Message (`messages/`) | Response (`llm/response/`) | Client wraps at |
|---|---|---|---|
| OpenAI | `openai_message.rb:6` | `openai_response.rb:4` | `openai.rb:148` |
| Anthropic | `anthropic_message.rb:6` | `anthropic_response.rb:4` | `anthropic.rb:126` |
| AwsBedrockAnthropic | **reuses** `AnthropicMessage` (adapter inherits `build_message`) | `aws_bedrock_anthropic_response.rb` | `aws_bedrock.rb` parse |
| GoogleGemini / VertexAI | `google_gemini_message.rb:6` | `google_gemini_response.rb` | gemini/vertex clients |
| MistralAI | `mistralai_message.rb:6` | `mistralai_response.rb` | `mistralai.rb:42` |
| Ollama | `ollama_message.rb:6` | `ollama_response.rb` | ollama client |

**Response base** `response/base_response.rb`: `raw_response`/`model` `:6`, abstract `chat_completion` `:35`, `completion` `:27`, `prompt_tokens/completion_tokens/total_tokens` `:70-86`. **Note:** `role` and `tool_calls` are **not** declared in the base, yet the assistant reads both (`assistant.rb:305`) — OpenAIResponse defines them at `:19`/`:27`, AnthropicResponse at `:55`/`:18`. A new tool-calling response that omits `role`/`tool_calls` silently breaks.

## Step 5 — Raw client family
